moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Mieses Opening, an unconventional first move that prepares to support our center while keeping maximum flexibility. d3 opens the long diagonal for our light-squared bishop and prepares e4 without committing to it immediately, giving us a quiet, positional setup that avoids sharp theoretical lines. This move appeals to players who prefer maneuvering and understanding position over memorizing opening theory.

Black takes a hypermodern approach, preparing to control the center from afar with a fianchettoed bishop on g7. By playing g6, Black is flexible and not committing to a specific pawn structure yet, while preparing to post the bishop on the long diagonal where it'll eye our center and queenside. This is a solid, patient response that respects our quiet opening while building a solid defensive setup.

This is the Myers Spike Attack—an aggressive thrust that immediately contests Black's fianchetto plan and stakes space on the kingside. g4 supports a potential h4 advance and prevents Black's bishop from settling comfortably on g7 by controlling g4 and g5; it's characteristic of this line's attacking intent despite the slow opening. We're gambling that kingside aggression will trouble Black more than the weakening of our kingside, so you need to follow up with concrete attacking plans rather than slow maneuvering.
